children.value: In this assignment you will write your first SQL queries against a manga library database. The goal is to get comfortable with the core building blocks of SQL: selecting data, filtering it, and sorting it.
You will work with a PostgreSQL database running locally on your machine via Docker, write your queries in VSCode, and submit your work through GitHub Classroom.
By the end of this assignment you will be able to:
statements to retrieve specific columns from a table
to filter rows based on conditions
to sort results in ascending and descending order
Year the first volume was published
to get your environment ready. Once set up, you should be able to:
Connect to it in VSCode via the SQLTools extension
Run queries directly in the editor
). Open each file, read the instructions in the comment block, and write your query below the comment.
List all manga titles, ordered alphabetically
Show the title and author of every manga
List all distinct genres in the database
Show titles and average ratings, ordered highest to lowest
List titles of all manga with status
Show titles and volumes of manga with more than 20 volumes, ordered by volumes descending
List titles and year published of manga published in 2010 or later
Show all manga with an average rating of exactly 5.0
List titles and genres of all manga in the
List all distinct statuses in the database
Show title, author, and year of manga published before 2000, ordered by year ascending
List titles, ratings, and volumes of manga with rating above 4.0 AND more than 10 volumes
Show titles of manga whose title starts with the letter
List titles and volumes of manga that have exactly 1 volume
Show titles and genres of manga that are NOT in the
List titles and year of manga published between 2000 and 2010 (inclusive), ordered by year
Show titles and ratings of manga with a rating between 3.0 and 4.0 (inclusive), ordered by rating descending
List titles and authors of manga where the author’s name contains
Show titles, status, and ratings of manga that are either
OR have a rating above 4.8, ordered by title
locally before submitting to see your score
You can push as many times as you want — each push triggers a new grading run
If you accidentally modify the data, reset the database with:
docker compose down -v && docker compose up -d
If a query returns no rows, double-check your
conditions — it may be correct, or it may be filtering too aggressively
When you are ready to submit:

git add .
git commit -m "completed hw01"
git push
Then go to your repository on GitHub →
→ click the latest run to see your score.
Your score is calculated automatically by the grader. Each question is worth
. Partial credit is not awarded per question — the result set must be correct.
The grader compares your result set against the expected output. This means:
A different but equivalent query will receive full marks as long as it produces the correct rows
Column order must match what is specified in the question
Extra or missing columns will result in 0 for that question
Questions? Use the feedback pull request on your repository or bring your error messages to office hours.
